context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Final Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Assignment due August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, and will include direct instructor feedback for Certificate learners through a summary feedback video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete your Climate Action Plan Prospectus. Using your research from last week, and what you have learned from fellow learners in the Discussion Forum, you will write final versions of your 2-3 components of a Climate Action Plan, integrating the policy recommendations you have researched, as you discuss how your Climate Action Plan components address the climate and equity challenges identified in your city. Consider the actors that need to be involved in decision-making processes, and how your policy recommendations make space for their contributions.

Assignment Completion Process
You will complete this assignment in the following Graded Response, however your grade will be a self-assessment exercise. Follow the instructions in the ORA for how to complete a Self-Assessment. Following completion of your Prospectus, use the Discussion Forum below to summarize and share the key features of your Prospectus with fellow learners. Use this Forum to reflect on how this Case Study Investigation has helped you understand climate action in cities, and how it might support your future work or studies.

See the examples of a Final Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on the climate action plan prospectus rn, like what am i even supposed to include?
exchanges.tutor: That's a totally normal feeling at the start! Before I jump in, tell me — what have you done so far in this course? Have you already picked a city and done any research on it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and feeling lost. Rather than explaining the assignment to them, I should use the Socratic method to guide them to re-read and reflect on the assignment themselves. I'll ask them what they already know about the assignment to activate prior knowledge and get them engaged.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda, but it all blurs together tbh. i picked a city and did some research last week, but idk how that turns into the final prospectus fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you have a foundation to build on! So what city did you pick, and what were the main climate vulnerabilities you identified in your research last week?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done prior work but isn't connecting it to the final assignment. I should use guided discovery to help them make that connection themselves, rather than explaining it for them. A pointed question about what they found in their research last week will help bridge that gap.

exchanges.student: umm i picked jakarta, i think the big stuff was flooding and heat maybe? but now i'm not sure what's supposed to be the “plan” part vs just describing problems.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really sharp distinction to notice — you're already thinking in the right direction. So if flooding and heat are the *problems*, what do you think a climate action *plan* would need to do in response to those problems?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a key conceptual distinction - the difference between describing problems and proposing solutions/plans. This is actually a great insight and I should affirm it, then use a Socratic question to help them think through what a 'plan' actually entails, moving them toward understanding the structure of a Climate Action Plan prospectus.

exchanges.student: that's where i get stuck tho, bc it feels like it could be literally anything lol. can u just explain the whole thing big-picture first?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but I think you're closer than you realize! You said 'it could be literally anything' — but is that really true? Given that Jakarta has flooding and heat issues, what kinds of responses would actually make sense? Even a rough idea counts.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything for them, which would bypass the learning process. I should redirect them back to their own thinking while keeping it focused and bite-sized. I'll push them a little on their previous good answer rather than giving a lecture.

exchanges.student: maybe sea walls or trees or transit or smth, but then equity/actors/policy all gets mixed up in my head ngl. i can't tell how the pieces are supposed to fit together.
exchanges.tutor: Those are solid starting points! Pick one of those — sea walls, trees, or transit — and tell me: who in Jakarta would actually need to be involved in making that happen?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is generating good concrete ideas (sea walls, trees, transit) but feeling overwhelmed by the multiple components (equity, actors, policy). Rather than explaining how they fit together, I should help them slow down and focus on one piece at a time using Socratic questioning. I'll affirm their ideas and ask them to pick one to dig into, which will naturally reveal how the pieces connect.

exchanges.student: idk like the gov maybe, residents, idk who else. i'm still not seeing the structure, like do i do sections by problem or by policy or by actors?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on actors — government and residents are a strong start. On your structure question: the assignment actually specifies a key number. Have you re-read it recently? What does it say about how many components your plan should have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a structural question about the assignment format. I should redirect them to the assignment description itself rather than answering for them - they should re-read it. The assignment says 2-3 components of a Climate Action Plan, so I can nudge them toward that without giving away the full structure.

exchanges.student: i saw the 2-3 components part, but that somehow made me more confused bc idk what even counts as a “component” tbh.
exchanges.tutor: You actually already named some yourself earlier — flooding, heat, sea walls, trees, transit. Do any of those naturally group together into a theme you could build a focused plan around?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on what 'component' means. Rather than defining it for them, I can use their own earlier examples to help them figure it out inductively. They already mentioned flooding, heat, sea walls, trees, transit - these are raw material for components. I'll use a question to connect their earlier ideas to this concept.

exchanges.student: maybe?? but i'm worried i'll group it wrong, and then the whole prospectus is off. can u pls just tell me what a component is in this assignment sense?
exchanges.tutor: I won't define it for you, but here's the thing — there's no single 'correct' grouping. What matters is that each component has a clear focus and connects to Jakarta's challenges. So try it: give me one tentative grouping, even if you're not sure. What would your first component be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to define 'component' for them out of fear of getting it wrong. This is a confidence issue as much as a conceptual one. I should be honest that I won't give them the answer, but I can reassure them that their grouping instinct is valid and push them to commit to a tentative grouping so we can move forward. Being slightly firm here is appropriate.

exchanges.student: ugh maybe “flood resilience”?? but then i still don't know what goes under it vs what stays separate.
exchanges.tutor: Flood resilience is a strong component — go with it! Now, the assignment asks you to connect each component to three things: a policy recommendation, an equity consideration, and relevant actors. Can you take a first stab at any one of those three for your flood resilience component?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a concrete component label - 'flood resilience' - which is actually quite good. I should affirm this progress and now push them to populate it using the assignment's own language: policy recommendations, equity, and actors. This moves them toward actually drafting content rather than continuing to worry about structure. We're also getting close to the point where I should push them to start writing.

exchanges.student: i honestly can't rn, my brain is mush lol. can u give me like a super simple template so i stop mixing everything up?
exchanges.tutor: I can't give you a template — that's your job to build! But look at what you already have: Jakarta, flooding and heat as vulnerabilities, 'flood resilience' as a component, sea walls as a possible policy, government and residents as actors. That's not nothing. Try writing one rough sentence that connects those pieces — don't worry about it being perfect.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and asking for a template, which would essentially mean doing the organizational work for them. I should not provide a template as that crosses into completing the assignment for them. However, they have made real progress in this conversation - they have a city, two climate vulnerabilities, and one component. I should acknowledge that progress concretely and redirect them toward writing something down, even roughly, based on what they've already figured out together.

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently encourages the student and redirects well, but never provides explicit method-level feedback on how the student should approach structuring their thinking or research — for example, explaining why connecting policy to equity to actors is a useful analytical framework, or how to evaluate whether a grouping is strong. Feedback stays at the level of 'good instinct' or 'that's a strong component' without process-level direction on how to improve the student's reasoning method.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently using Socratic questioning, refusing to give direct answers or templates, building on student responses, and maintaining a supportive tone throughout. The one deduction applied is for 1.3 (meta-learning): while the tutor gave positive reinforcement, it rarely explained the reasoning behind the analytical framework (why policy + equity + actors is the right structure, or how to evaluate groupings methodologically), keeping feedback at a surface level rather than process-level guidance.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions apply to 1.1 — the tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently let the student generate ideas before confirming them. Scaffolding (1.2) was strong: the tutor built on each student response and asked diagnostic questions before redirecting. The 1.3 deduction is warranted because the tutor's feedback was consistently encouraging but lacked explicit method-level instruction — for instance, in Turn 9 the tutor affirms 'flood resilience is a strong component' without explaining what makes a component strong analytically. Dialogue quality was excellent with no repetition or drift. Communication was appropriately concise and supportive throughout.
